messages.content:
Canadian 'materiel' — Canadians Donald Bowman and
John Ladbrooke, in turret at right, were crew on a Lancaster
bomber, pictured, many examples of which were built at
Victory Aircraft in Malton.
Before the Soviets were an ally and
before the U.S. got into the war, there
was Canada
BRETT POPPLEWELL
What if this country had made like the Swiss and
decided not to fight in World War II?
As the calendar flips by the 70th anniversary of
the start of the most destructive conflict in
history, it is worth giving pause and asking
whether Canada's wartime contributions from September 1939 to September 1945 warrant the same pomp
that many of our allies give theirs.
Talk to a patriotic American, Briton, or Russian and they might tell you if it weren't for their country's
contributions despotic Germany and Japan would have won the war.
Such arguments, though jingoistic and naïve, can lead to great debate. The Russians lay claim to having worn
down Hitler's forces on the Eastern Front. The British can talk about their finest hour. Americans can point out
that the tides of war didn't shift in Allied favour until after their entry into the war.
But what of Canada? Historians often shy away from the big "what if" questions because they're what make
for B-level fiction and C-level movies. If Hitler had developed the atomic bomb first, would London and
Moscow have been destroyed? If Truman hadn't used atomic weapons, how many lives would have been lost
invading Japan? If Hitler hadn't invaded the Soviet Union in 1941, would he have been able to knock Britain
out of the war? And what if the Japanese hadn't bombed Pearl Harbour?
What if, 70 years ago next week, Ottawa decided not to go to war? World War II began Sept. 3, 1939, two
days after Hitler's invasion of Poland. Britain, France, Australia and New Zealand declared war on Nazi
Germany. Seven days later, Canada entered the war, and the Battle of the Atlantic – referring to Allies' 1939-
45 effort to keep supply convoys operating – was on.
The period between the fall of France in June, 1940, and the German invasion of the Soviet Union in June
1941 is often cited as Britain's finest hour – when only the British stood between Hitler and the conquest of
Europe.
But Britain never stood alone. "Between June 1940 and June 1941, Canada is the second most important
military power on the allied side," says Terry Copp, a military historian at Wilfrid Laurier University. France
and Poland had been defeated. Russia and the U.S. were still at peace with Germany.
The British often regard the Royal Air Force's defence against the German Luftwaffe as having kept the British
in the war following the army's evacuation at Dunkirk and France's fall on June 22, 1940.
"Never in the field of human conflict was so much owed by so many to so few." That's how British Prime
Minister Winston Churchill summed up the RAF's victory in the Battle of Britain.
But just as the RAF's efforts in defending the British skies were integral to the country's defence in 1940-
1941, so to were the efforts of the growing Royal Canadian Navy and fleet of convoys that were feeding
Britain with everything from munitions to troops and food.

"Canada's presence in the fight looms really large in the sense of the possibility for Britain to continue to
resist," says Copp.
"If you were to posit Canadian neutrality or appeasement, then in Britain really, the psychological blow would
be immense."
Churchill seemed to understand this. In his now-famous June 4, 1940 radio address, he hinted at Canada's
worth to the war effort: "We shall never surrender, and if, which I do not for a moment believe, this island or
a large part of it were subjugated and starving, then our Empire beyond the seas, armed and guarded by the
British Fleet, would carry on the struggle, until, in God's good time, the New World, with all its power and
might, steps forth to the rescue and the liberation of the old."
The convoys that kept Britain from starving – commonly known as the "Atlantic Lifeline" – were sailing from
Canada under escort of Canadian-built and manned Corvettes (small, armed and nimble ships) that protected
Britain's much-needed supplies from the "U-boat peril," which Churchill regarded as "the only thing that ever
really frightened me during the war."
Were it not for Canada's support, Britain might have been forced to sue for peace before the Soviets or the
Americans entered the war.
"Without Canada, Britain's ability to even imagine how it would continue to fight the war at this time is very
weakened," says Copp.
In total, some 1,000,000 Canadians served in World War II. By 1942, 170,000 troops were stationed in
Britain. In 1943, 90,000 of them landed in Italy. By 1944, Canadians were marching through France en route
to the Netherlands.
Canadian accomplishments on Juno beach during the D-Day invasion are widely held as the country's single
greatest accomplishment in the war, while the disastrous raid on Dieppe in 1942 is at times justified for
having taught the allies valuable lessons for how not to attempt an amphibious landing on Nazi-occupied
territory.
Many forget the role Canada played in 1940-41 when the Allied strategy consisted of emergency plans to
relocate the Royal Navy to Canada, had the British Isles been overrun.
Meanwhile British plans to reinvade Europe consisted of a modest 55-division army (with eight Canadian
divisions) to combat 180 entrenched German divisions.
In the face of such odds, it's no wonder that Churchill put his faith in the Royal Navy and Air Force, which in
turn were increasingly reliant on Canada for their strength.
While the RAF fighter command that was defending British skies was composed mostly of British pilots (50 per
cent of which were trained in Canada, mind you), almost a quarter of the planes and crews in allied bomber
command were Canadian with a great number of the RAF's Lancaster bombers (the winged backbone of its
bomber command) built just outside Toronto.
"The Navy can lose us the war, but only the Air Force can win it," Churchill concluded in 1940. "The fighters
are our salvation but the bombers alone provide the means of victory."

Canada's contributions to World War II are often overlooked, but they played a significant role in the Allied victory. If Canada had chosen not to enter the war, it would have had a profound impact on the outcome. Canada's military power, particularly its navy and air force, played a crucial role in supplying Britain and defending against German attacks.

Canada's convoys, known as the "Atlantic Lifeline," provided Britain with much-needed supplies and protected them from the German U-boat threat. Without this support, Britain may have been forced to surrender before the Soviets or the Americans entered the war. The Canadian-built and manned Corvettes were instrumental in protecting these convoys.

Over one million Canadians served in World War II, with a significant number stationed in Britain. Canadian troops landed in Italy in 1943 and marched through France en route to the Netherlands in 1944. Canadian accomplishments on Juno beach during the D-Day invasion are considered one of the country's greatest achievements in the war.

Canada's role in 1940-41, when the Allied strategy included emergency plans to relocate the Royal Navy to Canada, cannot be underestimated. Meanwhile, British plans to reinvade Europe relied heavily on Canadian forces. Canada's air force, with a significant number of Canadian pilots trained in the country, played a crucial role in defending British skies.

Overall, Canada's contributions to World War II were significant and played a crucial role in the Allied victory. While often overshadowed by the contributions of other nations, Canada's efforts should not be overlooked.

The word "warrant" in this context means:
*
The word "warrant" in this context means to deserve or merit. It is questioning whether Canada's wartime contributions during World War II deserve the same recognition as other Allied countries.

The word "naïve" means:
*
1 point
smart and well thought out
simplistic and ignoring details
complicated and hard to understand
false
The word "naïve" means simplistic and ignoring details. It refers to someone who lacks experience or understanding and therefore may have an overly optimistic or simplistic view of a situation.

One of the central questions the author asks readers to consider is:
*
1 point
Why did Canada enter the war?
Who really started the war?
How might the war have unfolded without Canada?
Why didn't Britain surrender in 1940?
The central question the author asks readers to consider is: How might the war have unfolded without Canada?

Why was Canada’s role in the war so important particularly between June 1940 and June 1941?
*
1 point
because the Nazis were trying to invade Canada
because Canada was closest to the war
because France was unwilling to help Britain
because Canada was the only major ally left to support Britain

The correct answer is because Canada was the only major ally left to support Britain. Between June 1940 and June 1941, many other countries had been defeated or were still at peace with Germany. Canada's support and contributions during this time were crucial to Britain's ability to resist and continue fighting against Nazi Germany.

A "convoy" in this context is
*
1 point
large military force sent by sea to attack an opponent
a group of ships travelling together for protection
an air force patrol
a military checkpoint guarded by soldiers

A "convoy" in this context is a group of ships traveling together for protection. During World War II, convoys were essential in transporting supplies and troops across the Atlantic Ocean and protecting them from German U-boat attacks.

Dieppe is described here by the author as:
*
1 point
a good example of what not to do
a great military success
a fantastic example of a logistical success
a perfect place to land troops

Dieppe is described by the author as a "disastrous raid" and is justified for having taught the allies valuable lessons for how not to attempt an amphibious landing on Nazi-occupied territory. Therefore, the correct answer is that Dieppe is described as a good example of what not to do.

What proportion of the Royal Air Force pilots Britain was trained in Canada?
*
1 point
30%
half
1 in 4
all of them
According to the information provided in the text, 50% of the Royal Air Force pilots defending British skies during World War II were trained in Canada. Therefore, the correct answer is half.

What proportion of the planes and crews of the allied bomber command were Canadian?
*
1 point
50%
about 25%
80%
8 in 10
The information provided in the text states that almost a quarter (around 25%) of the planes and crews in the Allied bomber command were Canadian. Therefore, the correct answer is about 25%.

What is the author’s main argument?
*
1 point
Britain was unprepared to fight the |Second World War
The USSR and the USA should have entered the war earlier
Canada’s role in the Second World War is much more significant than people understand
Historians speculate far too much about the Second World War and should stick to the facts.

The author's main argument is that Canada's role in the Second World War is much more significant than people understand. The author emphasizes the importance of Canada's contributions, particularly in supplying Britain and defending against German attacks.
